food_subgroup: Seafood snacks / fried squid ball skewer

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general squid ball entry (FOODON_03315763); no specific entry exists for the skewered fried variant sold by Old Chang Kee. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers squid and cuttlefish products broadly; no specific code for processed squid ball skewer exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for squid ball skewer snack food.

serving.natural_language_portion: One serving is one stick containing approximately 2 to 3 squid balls, weighing approximately 50 g, as typically sold at Old Chang Kee outlets in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Fish ball, fried (per 100 g) — adapted for squid ball composition; HPB Singapore Foodo / USDA FoodData Central squid and fish ball entries used as compositional anchors
nutrient_source.organization: Health Promotion Board Singapore (HPB) / USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact HPB Foodo entry for 'sotong ball on a stick' was found. Values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried fish ball / squid ball entries and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central entry for squid (cooked) and fish ball (fried). The product is a processed squid-and-fish-paste ball, deep-fried, on a skewer. Macros reflect the fried, starch-bound squid ball matrix. Sodium is elevated due to seasoning and processing. Values represent a best-estimate composite for this product category.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of fish ball and processed seafood ball products — analogous estimate based on fried fish ball GI data; University of Sydney GI Database and peer-reviewed literature on starch-bound seafood products
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for sotong ball on a stick. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogy with fried fish ball and processed starch-bound seafood products. The University of Sydney GI Database lists similar processed seafood ball products in the low-to-medium GI range (GI 45–60). The starch binder (tapioca/wheat starch) and protein matrix moderate the glycemic response. GL per serving calculated as (52 × 8.25 g available carbohydrate per 50 g serving) / 100 = 4.3.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (~520 mg per 100 g; ~260 mg per 50 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit consumption of sotong ball on a stick due to its high sodium content from processing and seasoning; one stick provides approximately 260 mg sodium, representing over 10% of the recommended daily limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate content with low GI (estimated 52) due to starch-protein matrix
type2_diabetes.guidance: Sotong ball on a stick has a low estimated glycemic index (~52) and moderate glycemic load (~4.3 per serving), making it a relatively acceptable occasional snack for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when portion size is controlled to one stick.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Deep-fried preparation contributes to total fat and saturated fat; elevated sodium from processing
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried preparation of sotong ball on a stick contributes to total fat (~7.8 g/100 g) and saturated fat (~1.9 g/100 g), and the high sodium content (~520 mg/100 g) is a concern for cardiovascular health; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency and portion size.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sotong Ball On A Stick (also known as Sotong Ball OnStik) is a popular Singaporean street food snack sold by Old Chang Kee and similar local snack chains. It consists of 2 to 3 processed squid (sotong) balls made from a mixture of minced squid, fish paste, tapioca starch, and seasoning, shaped into balls and deep-fried until golden and crispy on the outside, with a chewy, springy interior. The balls are skewered on a wooden stick for convenient on-the-go eating. The snack is typically priced around SGD 2.40 per stick. Nutritionally, each 50 g serving provides approximately 87.5 kcal, 5.25 g protein, 3.9 g fat, and 8.25 g carbohydrate. The sodium content is notably high (~260 mg per serving) due to processing and seasoning, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index is low (~52), so the glycemic load per serving is moderate (~4.3), which is relatively manageable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderation. The snack is halal-certified and dairy-free. It is a culturally iconic Singaporean snack, widely available at hawker centres, shopping mall kiosks, and food courts across Singapore.
common_user_queries: How many calories in a sotong ball on a stick?
What is sotong ball made of?
How much sodium in sotong ball?
Old Chang Kee sotong ball nutrition

confidence_reason: No exact HPB Foodo or USDA entry for 'sotong ball on a stick' as a branded Old Chang Kee product was found. Nutrient values are adapted from HPB Singapore Foodo fried fish ball and squid ball entries and USDA FoodData Central squid (cooked) data. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database for similar processed seafood ball products. Confidence is medium due to the adapted rather than exact source match.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the HPB Foodo or USDA source entries used for this adapted estimate. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is -1 as it is not reported in the source entries. All other -1 values reflect nutrients not reported in the adapted source basis. No core macro is zero; all macros are non-zero and consistent with a fried, starch-bound squid ball product. GI estimated at 52 (low) based on analogy with fried fish ball and processed starch-bound seafood products from the University of Sydney GI Database. GL per serving = (52 × 8.25) / 100 = 4.3. The food_id GD001519 is preserved from the input seed record. The cuisine tag 'Curry Puff' in the input is a field survey platform category artefact and does not reflect the actual cuisine of this product; cuisine has been corrected to 'Singaporean Street Food'.
